latestPlannerResponse.message: I've updated my understanding, scope, and acceptance criteria based on our discussion.
latestPlannerResponse.featureUnderstanding: On the Rivvy Portal Login screen (/login) and Portal API, sign-in must deny authentication for UserAccount records with status disabled or invited, returning a unified 401 response with a single generic, non-enumerative error message. A Remember me control on the Login UI (unchecked by default) enables extended sessions: when selected, authenticated sessions persist for up to 180 days of inactivity (rolling). If an account is disabled after sign-in, all existing sessions are immediately invalidated upon the next request. No additional audit store beyond application logs is required.

in: Blocking sign-in for disabled and invited UserAccount records with a single generic 401 response via Portal API and reflected in the UI
Remember me option on the Login screen (unchecked by default) that extends session inactivity timeout to 180 days with rolling refresh
Immediate session invalidation behavior when a user becomes disabled (enforced by auth middleware on subsequent requests)
Consistent generic error messaging for disabled/invited sign-in attempts
out: General invalid-credential enumeration-safety and temporary lockout (handled by sibling story)
Password reset/forgot password flows and emails
CAPTCHA, device/IP-based rate limiting, or broader security hardening
UI redesign beyond adding Remember me and showing a generic error
Dedicated audit storage beyond application logs

assumptions: UserAccount.status values active|invited|disabled are authoritative for eligibility.
Generic error string: "Invalid email or password." returned with HTTP 401 for disabled/invited attempts.
Remember me is a rolling inactivity timeout of 180 days (no separate absolute lifetime cap).
Non-Remember me sessions retain the existing 8-hour inactivity timeout from the completed sign-in story.
Session storage already exists from the completed sign-in story; this change only adjusts TTL behavior and adds status checks.
Session invalidation on disablement is acceptable to enforce at request time via auth middleware (and optionally via a helper to revoke stored sessions when available).
Remember me applies to all roles.
acceptanceCriteria: Given a disabled user account, when correct credentials are submitted on /login, then the API responds 401 with a single generic error message, no session is created, and the UI shows the same generic message.

Given an invited (not yet activated) user account, when correct credentials are submitted, then the API responds 401 with the same generic error message, no session is created, and the UI shows the same generic message.
Given any disabled or invited account, when credentials are incorrect, then the API still responds 401 with the same generic error message and the UI shows the same generic message.
Given an active account and Remember me is unchecked at sign-in, then the session follows the existing 8-hour inactivity timeout (unchanged baseline).

Given an active account and Remember me is checked at sign-in, then the authenticated session remains valid across browser restarts and inactivity up to 180 days; if inactivity exceeds 180 days, the user must sign in again.
Given Remember me is checked, when the user closes and reopens the browser within 180 days, then the user remains signed in without re-authenticating.

Given an active session (with or without Remember me), when the user’s account status changes to disabled, then all of the user’s active sessions are revoked on the next request and subsequent authenticated requests return 401, requiring sign-in.

Given disabled or invited sign-in attempts, when observing API responses, then all such attempts return HTTP 401 with the same generic error payload (no reason codes or fields indicating disabled/invited).
Given the Login screen, when it renders, then a Remember me checkbox is displayed and is unchecked by default.

implementationPlan.planTitle: Implementation Plan for Disabled account handling

increments.title: Block disabled/invited sign-ins and add Remember me (180-day rolling session) with session invalidation on disablement
increments.intent:
- Backend (Portal API, Rivvy Portal Services):
  - Login handler updates:
    - Before creating a session, check UserAccount.status for the looked-up user.
    - If status != active (i.e., disabled or invited), return HTTP 401 with body { message: "Invalid email or password." } and do NOT disclose status.
    - Ensure this path does not emit different error shapes from general invalid-credential errors (sibling story will unify others).
    - Accept a boolean rememberMe in the login request payload. On successful authentication:
      - If rememberMe=false (or missing), create session with inactivity TTL=8 hours (existing default).
      - If rememberMe=true, create session with inactivity TTL=180 days. Implement as rolling/slide-on-activity (refresh TTL on each authenticated request).
    - Logging: On disabled/invited sign-in block, log at INFO with userId/email (if present) and reason in server logs only; avoid leaking reason to client.
  - Session management updates:
    - Add config settings: SESSION_IDLE_TTL_HOURS=8 (existing), REMEMBER_ME_IDLE_TTL_DAYS=180.
    - When issuing cookies, set Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite=Lax (or existing policy). For rememberMe=true, set cookie Max-Age to 180 days; otherwise set to session cookie or 8h Max-Age aligned with server TTL.
    - Ensure rolling inactivity is enforced server-side: on each authenticated request, extend TTL to now + selected inactivity window; if using server-side session store, refresh TTL; if using stateless JWT, switch to server-side or use refresh-token strategy (prefer existing server-side session from completed sign-in).
  - Auth middleware/guard:
    - On every authenticated request, load the session and resolve the current user.
    - Query the user_account table (by userId in session) to verify status is active. If not active:
      - Invalidate the session server-side (delete from store) and clear the cookie via Set-Cookie Max-Age=0.
      - Return HTTP 401; UI should redirect to /login via existing global auth handling.
    - Refresh session TTL according to whether the session was created as Remember me or standard (store a flag in session to distinguish).
  - Optional helper for proactive revocation:
    - Implement SessionService.invalidateAllForUser(userId) to allow admin flows to revoke sessions on status change events. This story does not wire admin UI, but provides the function for immediate reuse.
- Frontend (Rivvy Portal UI):
  - Login screen (/login) updates:
    - Add a Remember me checkbox (unchecked by default). Persist only in form state (no local storage needed).
    - Include rememberMe boolean in the login POST payload.
    - On any 401 from login, display the generic error message returned by API (e.g., "Invalid email or password."). Do not branch on hidden reasons.
  - Auth client:
    - Ensure it stores and forwards the session cookie (HttpOnly/secure handled by browser); no token storage in JS.
    - Ensure global auth handling routes users to /login on 401 responses from protected API calls (existing behavior likely present).
- Configuration:
  - Add environment variables with sane defaults:
    - SESSION_IDLE_TTL_HOURS=8
    - REMEMBER_ME_IDLE_TTL_DAYS=180
  - Document security cookie flags (Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite) and production HTTPS requirement.
- Data model:
  - No schema changes. Use existing user_account.status (active|invited|disabled).
- Observability:
  - Log disabled/invited sign-in blocks and session invalidations with correlation IDs. Avoid logging raw passwords.
- Tests (map to acceptance criteria):
  - Backend unit/integration tests:
    - Login with disabled account (correct password) returns 401 with generic message; no session created.
    - Login with invited account (correct password) returns 401 with generic message; no session created.
    - Login with disabled/invited account (wrong password) still returns 401 generic.
    - Successful login with rememberMe=false issues session with ~8h TTL; with rememberMe=true issues session with ~180d TTL (assert cookie Max-Age/Expires and server store TTL if accessible).
    - Auth middleware denies requests if user is disabled after session creation; session removed and 401 returned.
    - Rolling inactivity: advancing time and making an authenticated request refreshes TTL for both standard and remember-me sessions appropriately.
  - Frontend tests:
    - Login form renders Remember me unchecked by default.
    - Submitting with Remember me true sends rememberMe: true in payload.
    - On 401 from login, UI displays generic error.
    - With Remember me session, reload app and confirm user remains signed in (mock API) within TTL window.
- Deployment/rollback:
  - Backward compatible: sessions without remember-me flag default to 8h.
  - Rollback safe: revert code; existing sessions continue per their stored TTLs.
- Documentation:
  - Update developer docs and Portal API auth notes to mention disabled/invited behavior (401 generic) and Remember me 180-day rolling sessions.

answers.5f913338-df0c-455a-a612-06314cb3aab1: focusing only on disabled accounts plus Remember me
answers.91b814e8-cf8c-4a29-880b-6a3c8597d9c7: always 401 with a single generic message
answers.c7913b69-f43c-4785-8af9-bdc8790c6acf: unchecked by default and shown in Login screen
answers.54f03110-49ec-4926-8f3f-cc264348b1b1: blocked with the same generic response until activation

messages.content: Welcome! We’re set to work on “Disabled account handling” for Email/Password Sign-In. I have the product backlog and full architecture context available. Are there any specific architecture entities or diagrams you’d like to highlight as especially relevant for this story?
I've provided the architectural context, please give me your understanding of this feature
Here’s my current understanding focused on disabled accounts and Remember me; I have 8 questions to confirm scope and behavior.

Answers to your questions:

Q: To confirm scope: should this story exclude general invalid-credential enumeration-safety and temporary lockout (handled by the sibling), focusing only on disabled accounts plus Remember me?
A: focusing only on disabled accounts plus Remember me

Q: What HTTP status and exact generic message should be used for disabled attempts (e.g., always 401 with a single generic message)?
A: always 401 with a single generic message

Q: What is the Remember me duration, and is it rolling on activity or a fixed absolute lifetime?
A: 180 days

Q: Should Remember me be opt-in (unchecked by default) and shown on the Login screen UI?
A: unchecked by default and shown in Login screen

Q: If an account becomes disabled while a session (including Remember me) is active, should existing sessions be immediately invalidated?
A: Yes

Q: Is an audit record beyond application logs required for disabled attempts, and if so, where should it live?
A: No

Q: Should invited accounts be allowed to sign in, or are they also blocked with the same generic response until activation?
A: blocked with the same generic response until activation

Q: Any specific UI copy or design constraints for the generic error and Remember me label/help text?
A: None
I've updated my understanding, scope, and acceptance criteria based on our discussion.
Okay, let's see if we need to break this work item into parts...
This item is small enough, so let's get the software developer to review this.
Now let's get the Test Engineer to review this and define the test plan...

Unit tests cover backend login/middleware logic and session TTL behavior; functional tests cover UI flows with mocked APIs for error messaging and Remember me persistence. All acceptance criteria for disabled/invited handling and Remember me are mapped.

latestTestPlannerResponse.message: Unit tests cover backend login/middleware logic and session TTL behavior; functional tests cover UI flows with mocked APIs for error messaging and Remember me persistence. All acceptance criteria for disabled/invited handling and Remember me are mapped.

testPlan.title: LoginController returns 401 generic for disabled user with correct password
testPlan.description: JUnit5/Mockito: Given a user with status=disabled and valid password, when POST /login is handled, then the controller returns HTTP 401 with body { message: "Invalid email or password." } and SessionService is not invoked to create a session.

testPlan.title: LoginController returns 401 generic for invited user with correct password
testPlan.description: JUnit5/Mockito: Given a user with status=invited and valid password, when POST /login is handled, then the controller returns HTTP 401 with the same generic message and does not create a session.

testPlan.title: LoginController returns 401 generic for disabled user with incorrect password
testPlan.description: JUnit5/Mockito: Given a user with status=disabled and an incorrect password, when POST /login is handled, then the controller still returns HTTP 401 with the same generic message and no session is created.

testPlan.title: LoginController returns 401 generic for invited user with incorrect password
testPlan.description: JUnit5/Mockito: Given a user with status=invited and an incorrect password, when POST /login is handled, then the controller still returns HTTP 401 with the same generic message and no session is created.

testPlan.title: LoginController issues standard session when Remember me is unchecked
testPlan.description: JUnit5/Mockito: Given an active user and rememberMe=false (or missing), when POST /login succeeds, then SessionService.createSession is called with inactivity TTL of 8 hours and rememberMe flag=false.

testPlan.title: LoginController issues 180-day rolling session and persistent cookie when Remember me is checked
testPlan.description: JUnit5/Mockito: Given an active user and rememberMe=true, when POST /login succeeds, then SessionService.createSession is called with inactivity TTL of 180 days and rememberMe flag=true, and the response includes Set-Cookie with Max-Age≈180 days and Secure/HttpOnly/SameSite policy.

testPlan.title: AuthMiddleware revokes session and returns 401 when user becomes disabled
testPlan.description: JUnit5/Mockito: Given a valid session for a user who is later marked disabled, when a protected endpoint is accessed, then middleware deletes the session from the store, sets Set-Cookie to expire the cookie (Max-Age=0), and the request is terminated with HTTP 401.

testPlan.title: AuthMiddleware refreshes TTL on activity for standard sessions (8h rolling)
testPlan.description: JUnit5/Mockito with a controllable Clock: Given a standard session (rememberMe=false), when a protected request is made near TTL expiration, then the middleware refreshes the session TTL to now+8h in the session store.

testPlan.title: AuthMiddleware refreshes TTL on activity for Remember me sessions (180d rolling)
testPlan.description: JUnit5/Mockito with a controllable Clock: Given a Remember me session (rememberMe=true), when a protected request is made, then the middleware refreshes the session TTL to now+180d in the session store.

testPlan.title: Disabled/invited error payload is generic and non-enumerative
testPlan.description: JUnit5: When login is denied due to status disabled or invited, the serialized error body only includes the field { message: "Invalid email or password." } with no reason codes, status fields, or extra properties.

testPlan.title: SessionService.invalidateAllForUser removes all sessions for a user
testPlan.description: JUnit5/Mockito: Given multiple active sessions for a user, when invalidateAllForUser(userId) is called, then all corresponding session records are removed from the session store and subsequent lookups fail.

testPlan.title: Login form renders Remember me unchecked by default
testPlan.description: Vitest + React Testing Library: When /login renders, the Remember me checkbox is present and its default checked state is false.

testPlan.title: Login form sends rememberMe=false when unchecked
testPlan.description: Vitest + RTL + MSW: Given the Remember me checkbox is left unchecked, when the user submits credentials, then the login POST payload includes rememberMe=false (or omits the flag per client contract) and the request completes.

testPlan.title: Login form sends rememberMe=true when checked
testPlan.description: Vitest + RTL + MSW: Given the Remember me checkbox is checked, when the user submits credentials, then the login POST payload includes rememberMe=true.

testPlan.title: Login UI displays generic error on 401 for disabled account
testPlan.description: Vitest + RTL + MSW: Mock POST /login to return 401 with { message: "Invalid email or password." }; when credentials for a disabled user are submitted, the UI surfaces the same generic error text without additional details.

testPlan.title: Login UI displays generic error on 401 for invited account
testPlan.description: Vitest + RTL + MSW: Mock POST /login to return 401 with { message: "Invalid email or password." }; when credentials for an invited user are submitted, the UI displays the same generic error.

testPlan.title: Login UI shows generic error for incorrect credentials on disabled/invited accounts
testPlan.description: Vitest + RTL + MSW: For disabled and invited users with incorrect passwords, mock POST /login to return 401 with the generic message; verify the UI shows the same message and does not display any reason code.

testPlan.title: Remember me session persists across app reloads within 180 days
testPlan.description: Vitest + RTL + MSW: After a successful login with rememberMe=true, unmount and remount the app; mock authenticated endpoints to honor existing session cookie and return 200; verify the user remains signed in without re-authentication.

testPlan.title: Standard session (Remember me off) respects 8-hour inactivity timeout
testPlan.description: Vitest + RTL + MSW: After a successful login with rememberMe=false, simulate an "inactive >8h" condition by having subsequent authenticated API calls return 401; verify the app routes to /login and prompts for sign-in again.

testPlan.title: Remember me session expires after 180 days of inactivity
testPlan.description: Vitest + RTL + MSW: After a successful login with rememberMe=true, simulate inactivity beyond 180 days by having the next authenticated API call return 401; verify the app routes to /login and requires re-authentication.

testPlan.title: Active session is revoked on disablement and subsequent requests return 401
testPlan.description: Vitest + RTL + MSW: With an active session (either mode), change MSW to return 401 for authenticated endpoints (simulating user status changed to disabled); on the next request, the UI redirects to /login and displays the generic error.

testPlan.title: API error payload consistency for disabled/invited attempts
testPlan.description: Vitest + MSW: Intercept POST /login responses for disabled and invited attempts; assert the client receives identical 401 payloads with only { message } and that the UI does not expose any status-specific hints.

specIntentTexts.INC-1:
/shape-spec ## Feature Description
On the Rivvy Portal Login screen (/login) and Portal API, sign-in must deny authentication for UserAccount records with status disabled or invited, returning a unified 401 response with a single generic, non-enumerative error message. A Remember me control on the Login UI (unchecked by default) enables extended sessions: when selected, authenticated sessions persist for up to 180 days of inactivity (rolling). If an account is disabled after sign-in, all existing sessions are immediately invalidated upon the next request. No additional audit store beyond application logs is required.

## In Scope
- Blocking sign-in for disabled and invited UserAccount records with a single generic 401 response via Portal API and reflected in the UI
- Remember me option on the Login screen (unchecked by default) that extends session inactivity timeout to 180 days with rolling refresh
- Immediate session invalidation behavior when a user becomes disabled (enforced by auth middleware on subsequent requests)
- Consistent generic error messaging for disabled/invited sign-in attempts

## Out of Scope
- General invalid-credential enumeration-safety and temporary lockout (handled by sibling story)
- Password reset/forgot password flows and emails
- SSO/OIDC flows
- CAPTCHA, device/IP-based rate limiting, or broader security hardening
- UI redesign beyond adding Remember me and showing a generic error
- Dedicated audit storage beyond application logs

## Sibling Items (handled separately — do NOT implement these)
- [COMPLETED] Sign in with valid credentials: As a user, I can successfully authenticate with a valid email and password, resulting in an authenticated session; post-authentication destination is handled by the role-based routing feature.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Given a valid, active user account with the correct email and password, when I submit the login form, then an authenticated session is established.
- Given I sign in successfully without selecting Remember me, when I am inactive for 8 consecutive hours, then my session expires and I must sign in again.
- Given I enter an email with leading or trailing whitespace, when I attempt to sign in, then the whitespace is ignored and the trimmed value is validated.
- Given I enter an email using different letter casing than stored, when I attempt to sign in, then the comparison is case-insensitive and I can still sign in if the credentials are correct.
- Post-authentication destination is handled by the Post-Login Role & Organization Routing feature and validated by its acceptance criteria.
- [IN_PROGRESS] Blocked and failed sign-in handling (enumeration-safe, lockout, disabled)

## Assumptions
- UserAccount.status values active|invited|disabled are authoritative for eligibility.
- Generic error string: "Invalid email or password." returned with HTTP 401 for disabled/invited attempts.
- Remember me is a rolling inactivity timeout of 180 days (no separate absolute lifetime cap).
- Non-Remember me sessions retain the existing 8-hour inactivity timeout from the completed sign-in story.
- Session storage already exists from the completed sign-in story; this change only adjusts TTL behavior and adds status checks.
- Session invalidation on disablement is acceptable to enforce at request time via auth middleware (and optionally via a helper to revoke stored sessions when available).
- Remember me applies to all roles.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Given a disabled user account, when correct credentials are submitted on /login, then the API responds 401 with a single generic error message, no session is created, and the UI shows the same generic message.
- Given an invited (not yet activated) user account, when correct credentials are submitted, then the API responds 401 with the same generic error message, no session is created, and the UI shows the same generic message.
- Given any disabled or invited account, when credentials are incorrect, then the API still responds 401 with the same generic error message and the UI shows the same generic message.
- Given an active account and Remember me is unchecked at sign-in, then the session follows the existing 8-hour inactivity timeout (unchanged baseline).
- Given an active account and Remember me is checked at sign-in, then the authenticated session remains valid across browser restarts and inactivity up to 180 days; if inactivity exceeds 180 days, the user must sign in again.
- Given Remember me is checked, when the user closes and reopens the browser within 180 days, then the user remains signed in without re-authenticating.
- Given an active session (with or without Remember me), when the user’s account status changes to disabled, then all of the user’s active sessions are revoked on the next request and subsequent authenticated requests return 401, requiring sign-in.
- Given disabled or invited sign-in attempts, when observing API responses, then all such attempts return HTTP 401 with the same generic error payload (no reason codes or fields indicating disabled/invited).
- Given the Login screen, when it renders, then a Remember me checkbox is displayed and is unchecked by default.

# Architecture Meta-Model Reference

This section describes the entity types and relationship types in the Architecture Meta-Model. Use this reference to understand the structure of the architecture model data that follows.

> **Note:** `ui_contracts` is excluded from the model data below. It is a deprecated duplicate of `interfaces` and is pending removal.

## Entity Types

### Application Architecture

- **`applications`** -- Top-level deployable systems
- **`app_components`** -- Human-defined grouping of service types/tiers/layers within an application on a technical basis (e.g. "Web Tier", "Persistence Tier"), so that certain important things can be stated about this grouping e.g. "our company uses React/TS for all frontends" + if a project has 3 services whose parent app_component is "UI Tier", then those 3 deployed services should be React/TS according to company technical standards.
- **`services`** -- Runtime components within an application
- **`interfaces`** -- API boundaries exposed by services
- **`endpoints`** -- Individual operations on an interface
- **`application_points`** -- **Polymorphic wrapper (1:1 with a concrete entity)**. Every application_point row maps to exactly one concrete entity (application, app_component, service, or interface). This mechanism allows relationships like `data_movements` to flexibly reference any level of the application architecture as source/target. **AUTO-MANAGED by the backend -- the LLM must NEVER create these directly.**

### Code-Level Entities

- **`classes`** -- Code-level entities. E.g. if Java, it's a Java class. Used in UML "Class diagrams".
- **`methods`** -- Code-level methods on classes. Used in "Class diagrams" and referenced in other diagrams e.g. a sequence diagram might show an internal calculation `calcMyNumbers(...)`.

### Data Entities

- **`logical_data_entities`** -- Conceptual data objects (DTOs), often representing the actual shape of data transferring between applications in APIs.
- **`logical_data_attributes`** -- Fields on logical data entities (name, dataType, isPrimaryKey, isNullable).
- **`physical_data_entities`** -- Persisted storage structures (database tables, collections, physical files).
- **`physical_data_attributes`** -- Columns on physical data entities (name, dataType, isPrimaryKey, isNullable).
- **`data_entity_points`** -- **Polymorphic wrapper (1:1 with a concrete entity)**. Every data_entity_point maps to exactly one logical or physical data entity. Allows relationships to flexibly reference either type. **AUTO-MANAGED by the backend -- the LLM must NEVER create these directly.**

### Business Domain

- **`business_users`** -- Actors/roles who use the system.
- **`business_processes`** -- High-level business workflows.
- **`process_activities`** -- Steps within a business process.
- **`business_points`** -- **Polymorphic wrapper (1:1 with a concrete entity)**. Every business_point maps to either a `business_process` or an `activity`. Allows relationships to reference either a very high-level business process or a medium-to-high level business activity. **AUTO-MANAGED by the backend -- the LLM must NEVER create these directly.**
- **`app_business_points`** -- **Super-polymorphic wrapper**. Allows the flexible choice of either an `application_point` or a `business_point` in a relationship. **AUTO-MANAGED by the backend -- the LLM must NEVER create these directly.**
- **`interactions`** -- A flexible definition allowing you to define any kind of interaction that a user may have with `application_points` or `business_points` (hence the use of `app_business_points`). Architecturally loose by design.

### State Machine

- **`events`** -- Events in a state machine model.
- **`states`** -- States in a state machine model.
- **`state_transitions`** -- Transitions between states in a state machine.

### Activity/Workflow

- **`activities`** -- Steps in an activity/workflow diagram.
- **`activity_flows`** -- Connections between activities in a workflow.
- **`activity_partitions`** -- Swimlanes/groupings in activity diagrams.

### UI Architecture

- **`ui_screens`** -- Frontend pages/views. Key attributes: **`route`** (the URL path pattern this screen is served at, e.g. `/dashboard`, `/projects/:id/settings`), **`description`** (purpose and content of the screen). When the architecture model includes UI screens, their `route` attribute tells you the exact URL path for navigation.
- **`ui_components`** -- Component/element within a screen (not a UI widget). What would be defined in multiple frontend frameworks in code in `src/components`. Components build on top of each other e.g. basic components are used in the definition of parent, more complex components. Key attributes: **`description`** (what the component renders/does).
- **`ui_actions`** -- User-triggered or system-triggered actions in UI. Key attributes: **`description`** (what happens when the action fires), **`trigger`** (what initiates the action, e.g. button click, form submit).
- **`ui_characteristics`** -- Business features / UX characteristics linked to services that are "UI tier" i.e. frontend UIs.

### Business Logic & Packaging

- **`business_logics`** -- Business rules/domain logic owned by a service.
- **`package_sets`** -- A set of "packages" that allows someone to state their full src directory/package structure.
- **`packages`** -- Code packaging (think Java Package, albeit this word is different in other languages e.g. Python Module).
- **`package_set_default_rules`** -- Auto-resolution rules for package set selection.

## Relationship Types

- **`business_user_business_points`** -- Links business users to business points (polymorphic: business processes or activities).
- **`application_point_business_points`** -- Links application points to business points.
- **`logical_data_entity_relationships`** -- Links between logical data entities (e.g. FK-style references, associations).
- **`logical_data_entity_physical_data_entities`** -- Maps logical entities to physical entities (logical<->physical mapping).
- **`logical_data_attribute_physical_data_attributes`** -- Maps logical attributes to physical attributes.
- **`data_movements`** -- Data flowing between application points, optionally referencing a data entity point or interface.
- **`interface_logical_entities`** -- Links interfaces to the logical entities they expose.
- **`ui_workflow_transitions`** -- Navigation/flow between UI screens.
- **`application_point_business_logics`** -- Links application points to business logic rules.

## Important Notes on Polymorphic Wrappers

The following entity types are **polymorphic wrappers** that are **automatically managed by the backend**. The LLM must **NEVER** create, update, or delete these entities directly. They are created and maintained automatically when their corresponding concrete entities are saved:

1. **`application_points`** -- Auto-created for applications, app_components, services, and interfaces
2. **`data_entity_points`** -- Auto-created for logical_data_entities and physical_data_entities
3. **`business_points`** -- Auto-created for business_processes and activities
4. **`app_business_points`** -- Auto-created to bridge application_points and business_points


## Architecture Context
Here is the part of the architecture that directly applies to this spec and it must be reviewed as part of the spec above. It is expressed as a JSON payload about business, application, data, behavioural and/or UI architecture:

{"entities":[{"id":1,"name":"RS UI Tier","type":"appComponents","fields":{"applicationId":"app-mlxwja0d-7swkq"}},{"id":2,"name":"RS Service Tier","type":"appComponents","fields":{"applicationId":"app-mlxwja0d-7swkq"}},{"id":3,"name":"RS Persistence Tier","type":"appComponents","fields":{"applicationId":"app-mlxwja0d-7swkq"}},{"id":4,"name":"AWS
